I've had the Game since early May and it ran fine for a few weeks. However, it randomly started crashing and freezing. The first time this happened was during a cut scene the audio continued playing, but the screen froze and the subtitles continued playing but were lagged out. Now the game will crash within at least half an hour of play time. My PC should run it fine, I've got a 9070xt, 48GB of RAM, A 7800X3D and a 2tb M.2 SSD. I'm not a particularly long way into the story because the game is unplayable. My CPU sits at 95%-100% usage the entire time I play it. It runs in 1440p with a mixture of high and ultra settings but just randomly freezes and it takes 15+ seconds for the game to even let me quit plus a further full minute for my PC to become usable again. No matter how many reports I seem to submit, nothing seems to be done about it. From the Steam Forums for the game I don't appear to be the only one with these problems, (if my memory serves correctly) people with RTX 5090s were also experiencing similar problems. It's not a graphics problem because I tried lowering settings to no avail and other games (like Black-myth Wukong) run at "cinematic" settings with max raytracing in native 1440p, (I also know this because for the 15-20 minutes it does run I have an average of 185 fps in spiderman 2). I don't know how to fix it so I've mostly avoided playing it, just checking up on it every few weeks.

I will update this comment if they patch this, because the game is otherwise worthwhile.

I got this game and was super excited to play it. I played it for about 10/47 minutes that it was running. First, it took forever just for the initial cutscene to load. If I wanted to watch a Spider-Man movie, I would just watch it. I wanted to play a game, not watch a movie.
Also, the controls were not very intuitive. And I know you can change them, but that takes forever, and then you have to fix the controls that you replaced with the reassignments which just takes longer.

Awesome game! Much faster swinging, super upgraded combat. Story has been great so far. We all know it's not optimized for PC (I'm not about to rattle off my specs - they're decent enough) but there's a quick fix on youtube (readjust the graphs from the launcher to very low, disable all visuals and then in the game set it all to medium or low if possible) that makes the game run smoothly.
I haven't had problems with it ever since (well, it crashed once but autosave worked well enough) and I don't mind the lower res (it's still pretty good even for low).
However, if you care about aesthetics, then yeah, maybe it won't run at full speed and you won't get that experience. But story-wise and mechanically, I do think this game is amazing.

🕷️ This isn’t just a superhero game. It’s the definition of what a sequel should be.

From the moment I slung into the skies of New York, I knew I was in for something special.

The web-swinging? Even smoother than the first game. It’s not traversal—it’s art.

The combat? So fluid and responsive, every punch felt like it was choreographed by a Marvel fight director.

The story? Gripping. Emotional. Personal. It balances two Spider-Men with heart and intensity without missing a beat.

Peter’s arc hits hard in ways I didn’t expect.
Miles continues to shine, not just as a hero—but as a human being trying to balance loss, identity, and legacy.
And the villains? Let's just say… they’re not just obstacles. They’re stories of their own.

Every mission feels intentional. Every scene feels cinematic.
The voice acting, the music, the visuals—top tier. You can feel the love Insomniac poured into this from start to finish.

Whether I was gliding through Harlem or facing one of the most intense boss fights in PlayStation history, I was completely immersed.

Spider-Man 2 is not just a game—it’s an experience.
It respects the fans. It pushes boundaries. It tells a story that matters.

10/10.

I don't get what people are saying about poor performance. I am comfortably hitting 60 FPS — mostly around 120 FPS although with a lot of variance — with a 9950X3D and RTX 4090. Seems like they're having a skill issue, idk.

Anyway, game is great. It's really what you'd expect of a AAA game with lots of time spent introducing and digging into characters while maintaining decent pacing. There's also some surprising details in the gameplay that you really don't expect but that help build the world.

It being on PC is also a nice bonus, you can still use a DualShock controller while not having the e-waste of an unupgradable and unrepairable console, plus you can use mods. Shoutout to Overstrike.

Marvel's Spider-Man 2 on PC: A Web-Slinging Masterpiece Soars to New Heights
Marvel's Spider-Man 2 swings onto PC with all the exhilarating action, heartfelt storytelling, and stunning visuals that made it a console hit, now enhanced with the power and flexibility of the PC platform. Insomniac Games, with Nixxes Software's expert optimization, has delivered a truly remarkable experience that not only lives up to its predecessors but elevates the superhero gaming genre to new, breathtaking levels. From the moment you launch yourself into the expanded New York City, it's clear this is a labor of love, offering an unparalleled adventure for fans and newcomers alike.

The core gameplay loop remains as addictive and polished as ever, with traversal being a particular highlight. Web-swinging through the city feels incredibly fluid and dynamic, now made even more exhilarating with the addition of the Web Wings, allowing for seamless transitions between high-speed aerial glides and intricate web maneuvers. Combat, too, has evolved, offering a deeper and more varied experience. Switching between Peter Parker's new, aggressive symbiote abilities and Miles Morales' electrifying bio-electric powers adds a strategic layer to every encounter, encouraging creative combos and satisfying takedowns against a diverse rogues' gallery.

Beyond the thrilling action, Marvel's Spider-Man 2 truly shines in its gripping narrative and compelling character development. The story masterfully balances the dual perspectives of Peter and Miles, exploring their individual struggles and the weight of their responsibilities as Spider-Men. The introduction of iconic villains like Kraven the Hunter and, most notably, Venom, is handled with incredible care, delivering impactful and emotionally resonant arcs. The voice acting is superb, bringing these beloved characters to life with nuance and intensity, making every cutscene and interaction feel genuinely impactful.

On the technical front, the PC port is nothing short of fantastic. Nixxes Software has once again proven their prowess, delivering a highly optimized experience that takes full advantage of PC hardware. With support for unlocked framerates, NVIDIA DLSS 3, AMD FSR 3.1, and Intel XeSS, players can tailor the visual fidelity and performance to their specific setups, ensuring smooth and stunning gameplay. Ray-traced reflections and shadows further immerse you in the vibrant world of Marvel's New York, while ultrawide monitor support provides an expansive, cinematic view of the action. The game looks and runs beautifully, making the already impressive visuals even more breathtaking.

In conclusion, Marvel's Spider-Man 2 on PC is an absolute must-play for anyone seeking an exceptional superhero adventure. It combines thrilling gameplay, a captivating story, and stellar PC optimization into a cohesive and unforgettable experience. The seamless character switching, expanded traversal options, and nuanced narrative make this a true highlight in gaming. Insomniac and Nixxes have set a new standard for superhero titles on PC, and I wholeheartedly recommend swinging into this incredible journey.

Absolute fan of spider man. Completed the first game twice and was very excited about this one. Overall the story is great, I liked it, though the story in the first game is better in my opinion.

Second line stories weren't that interesting, so I skipped half of them.

What I really liked is that the game changed some gameplay mechanics, at first it felt weird, but it is nice to have something new.

Having more city ares is fantastic. In the first game I always wanted to sit on a ship that sails, but it wasn't possible. Here you can do it :)

My main problem is that the game is still not fully optimized. Lags and freezes would happen quite often, still playable, but gets very annoying eventually. I have rtx 3070 and the game had almost all graphic setting on max from start, but due to lags and freezes I had to decrease it several times to the point where I was playing on low settings and it still would sometimes have performance issues, though significantly less. Overall it doesn't ruin the game (especially assuming it became a typical issue in game industry), but can be annoying.

I would say overall 8/10, but keep in mind that I am biased due to my love for spidey and this game series overall. Just hope that 3rd game will do better with optimization.

Overall, it's a decent Spider-Man game. Feels similar to the first Spider-Man but with a lot more bells & whistles that don't really make it better.

Pros:
I really liked they way they implemented both Spider-Men. You can easily swap between them and the other will sometimes join you in a fight.
The City and game are beautiful to look at. Feels like a living world.
Suits from prior movies and TV shows are implemented in a fun way.

Cons:
The story/writing is really bad. Things just happen without reason.
The new systems make the game unnecessarily complicated. Even at a regular difficulty, you need to make good use of all your skills and abilities to survive.
The game doesn't really feel expanded from the first one, just rehashed.

I have played this franchise before (the first two thanks to that woman holding a spoon) and I love them all! While this is the first Spider-Man I bought, I will not miss the opportunity to provide feedback. Everything is great, the plot, the progression, the dialogues, everything, except for one thing.

I have rx 5500xt right now, paired with amd ryzen 5 2600 and 16gb ram. I installed this game on an NVME SSD. I already put the dlss files to a new folder within the game's local folder to enhance the performance of the game. Thinking I have the bare minimum of the specs needed to run this game, I thought that it is normal for the game to 'pause and load' while transitioning to another area. I have already tweaked every possible configurations in the graphics menu, I even tried it at the lowest and now my settings are at custom ranging from low to medium, and I never had any issues, aside from TRAVERSAL STUTTER. The only problem I encounter is when moving to another area and it pauses and the loading icon appears. I had a few problems like I webslinged into an unrendered building, trapping me inside.

I read the community here about the lags and it seems that I was not the only experiencing the same issue, like those people even have better specs and yet they still experience the traversal stutter. Based on what I have also read, this issue can be fixed by the game creators.

Man, I love this game. I even 100% the Spider-man 1 and Miles Morales and even kept playing for hours after. Right now, I can't recommend this to anybody, but I'm willing to change it. I just wish Insomniac can do something about this.

Honestly, this is the disappointment of the year for me so far and will most likely keep that title throughout the rest of the year. Combat feels like a significant downgrade, with parries not meshing well with the combat, the removal of any interesting gadgets and the ones that remain feel bland to use. The rework how venom works for Miles is a massive letdown in my opinion, as well as the spider arms being generally underwhelming. Traversal could have been an improvement if it was just the enhanced web swinging with no swing assist, but the wingsuit and design of half the map let that down. The wingsuit is such a boring traversal method, being little more than simply gliding to the objective with little input from the player. In comparison, Just Cause 3 and 4 have great wingsuiting, with the grapple hook constantly keeping me engaged. Brooklyn and Queens are mostly dead air, with no areas to swing, and while some areas in are pretty nice it can't be saved from being an unfun forgettable area. The story also isn't up to snuff, especially in comparison with the first 2 games. The black suit is eyerolling and Venom honestly feels rushed. Miles on the other hand has pretty much nothing to do for 90% of the story despite being a playable character, and spends a solid 2/3 of the game complaining about Li. In addition to all of this, this version is an unoptimized mess, with me only able to keep a consistent frame rate on the second lowest setting with half the settings tweaked and ray tracing off. My computer is well above the recommended specs for the game, and other games of similar graphical fidelity running perfectly fine. Combine that with me encountering multiple glitches and crashes throughout my time playing, I can't in any faith recommend this game to anyone.

It's not bad, it's just extremely disappointing. The game feels like bloated DLC on what is already a tired formula.

Spider-man 1 was a breath of fresh air when it came out. It was a fully realized Spider-man experience and smart enough to streamline the lore so it could focus on some original ideas. It may have worn its inspirations on its sleeve, but it felt like there was genuine enthusiasm behind it.

Then came the DLC. Then came the Miles Morales game. Now we have a "fresh" sequel that feels like a corporate mandate instead of a passion project.

This game brings very few ideas to the table. The new features are shallow. The expanded map feels like more of the same. The 2 playable characters don't have any real gameplay differences between them. I can't help but feel like I've played this exact game 3 times already. You know they know it's bad when they give Mary Jane a gun and have you play as her. After the 2nd mandatory biking section, I was said "Is this really the best they could come up with for a Spider-man game? A biking mini-game with terrible controls?" The didn't know how to improve upon the formula and yet they made the game any way.

Each boss fight gets repeated 3-4x with little variation. I agree with whoever added in the new attack types that fighting gets stale after a while and needs more variety. But this feels half baked. I was consistently a quarter second late reacting to every visual cue and I don't think it's a me problem. It was easier to memorize attack animations than to rely on the cues. Out of all the notes they cripped from Arkham City, I don't know how you overlook the potential here for incredible supervillian boss fights. It's just more of the same, often literally.

The salt in the wound is the story. We've all seen the major beats of this story many times already. While I like the added wrinkle of having Miles and using Kraven's Last Hunt as inspiration, it's repetitive. It's like an episode of Paw Patrol where every character is defined by 1 thing and they have to remind you about what that 1 thing is in every sentence. Here's another scene about Kraven saying he wants a real fight. Here's yet another scene of Peter Parker complaining about how he doesn't have a choice. The worst is when you spend 10 minutes with Miles whining about how hard it is to balance his personal life followed by another 10 minutes with Peter whining about the exact same thing. It's twice the spider-angst stuffed into the same length of content, with nothing new to say.

On top of it all, the game is poorly optimized: framerate stuttering, visual glitches, a memory leak. In one level I played, Peter Parker became a blindingly bright light bulb. It looked like Spider-man's holy ghost was dishing out divine vengeance. A lot of random stuff that I gave up trying to troubleshoot. It didn't make the game unplayable for me, but it definitely diminished the experience.

They hint heavily at a Spider-man 3. I want it to be good, but if it's more of this I will only pick it up at a steep discount.

J.J.J. Reviews Spider-Man 2: A Glorified Web of Nonsense!
Daily Bugle Editorial – by THE J.J.J.

Ladies and gentlemen of New York — I’ve seen a lot in my day. Supervillains. Alien invasions. Gluten-free pizza. But nothing prepared me for the disappointment that is Marvel’s Spider-Man 2. I played it. I regretted it. And now I need therapy, coffee, and possibly a flamethrower.

🕷️ Two Spider-Men. Zero Brains.
Let’s talk about the “dynamic duo” — Peter Parker and Miles Morales. Two guys in spandex swinging around like caffeinated squirrels, wrecking half the city in the name of justice. And this time, the game expects us to care about their feelings. Feelings?! I don’t want an emotional support Spider-Man — I want a refund.

Venom shows up, broods like he’s auditioning for a teen vampire movie, and Kraven... oh, Kraven. The man walks around shirtless like he owns the jungle and mumbles vague threats like a discount Bond villain. The most dangerous thing about him is his man bun.

🕸️ Gameplay That’s As Fresh As Week-Old Pizza
Swinging around the city? Still fun — for the first hour. After that, it’s rinse, web, repeat. Punch a guy, launch a guy, throw a manhole cover, go home, cry about Uncle Ben — again.

And don’t get me started on side missions. At one point, I was helping a stray cat find its jazz club. A jazz club. I signed up to take down supervillains, not babysit Garfield on open mic night!

🐞 Glitches Galore
This game has more bugs than a summer picnic in Central Park. One minute I’m swinging through Harlem, the next I’m stuck halfway through a billboard of myself — which, frankly, was the only good part. The rest of the city looks like someone copy-pasted Brooklyn 400 times and hoped no one would notice.

🎮 Final Thoughts From Your Favorite Newsman
Spider-Man 2 is a lot like Spider-Man himself — flashy, overhyped, and more trouble than he's worth. The developers clearly spent more time rendering Peter's perfectly tousled hair than writing a story that made any sense.

Play it if you want, but don’t say I didn’t warn you. And if I see one more review calling it a "masterpiece," I’m launching a lawsuit for slander.

The animations, the fightin, all that jazz looks good, and I was liked it. But story-wise, I can't really say much 'cause I don't know squat about the Marvel universe, so it's hard for me to follow. But the story's probably aight if you're into that kinda thing.

Didn't really notice a lot of game breaking glitches some reviewers are mentioning during play. A few hiccups when trying to start the game, but just forced closed and restart worked. But also, when pausing the game during a cut scene, I noticed that sometimes the cut scene would continue to play, when normally you could pause the cinematic.

Overall, with 30 hours of play, probably twenty six, if we don't account for afk pause screen, this game was fantastic! The story telling was rich, the balance between two different characters and their unique abilities, chef's kiss. I'm glad I avoided looking up all the spoilers for the game as it was EPIC.

While I won't buy a PS4 when the next game releases, I sure as hell am excited to see what Insomniac has in store for their next game as it will be an instant buy when it releases on the PC.

As a whole, totally worth the money, whether on sale or not.

This game saved my life.

I am 3000 years old.

My ex-wife and I have a daughter together, and adopted our son together. They are now both 4 years old.

When we were going through our separation, I found myself lost and miserable. I was self destructive. I got so mad one day from everything spiralling out of my control that I punched some concrete in a moment of overwhelming emotion. That caused me to break my 5th metacarpal in my right hand... my working hand... my games hand.. the hand that I held and carried my children to bed with.. The hand I desperately needed to make sure I could continue to provide.

After learning of the severity of my self-inflicted damage, I was borderline suicidal. Keep in mind that just a few months before this, I was the happiest man with no history of depression or anxiety. I have never had fits of rage, or been one to break down and cry, but I was in a low spot that just really buried me from being able to see the light on the other side.

Having nothing better to do, I searched for a game I could play, ONE HANDED while I recovered. I somehow stumbled upon this game and read some of the reviews. I decided that it had to be worth a shot... I must admit, I didn't beat the game, or play nearly as long as some of you. In fact, I may have only played this game a day or two.. With that being said, after doing so, I had a new found joy and hope for life. I was able to put behind me the pain and suffering that had been cast over me. I was able to experience other peoples joy and happiness. I was able to see the fruits of my "labor". I relaxed for 5 ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ minutes to this music long enough to realize that I was going to be okay.

After coming to that realization, I turned the game off, and I went back to work. It hurt my hand like hell but I was motivated. I stopped feeling so damn sorry for myself, and I became the father I needed to be in that moment, not the weak boy I was behaving as.

Today, I am close friend with my kids mother. We don't fight, or argue, or say hurtful things to each other. We are parents, and friends.

I now have 3 kids. My third child is, wait for it, ALSO 4 YEARS OLD. The woman I am with was going through a very similar situation at the time of my own separation, and we just stumbled in to each others life unexpectedly. We have been in a relationship for a year now, and are very happy together.

Moral of the story, you never know what life holds in store for you, and if I would have given up when all the odds were stacked against me, I wouldn't be where I am today. This silly little game helped me realize that.

Thank you.

I think we all are agree that one of the best super hero games ever created is this series, we can argue about that Batman series released years ago but for now, this series is the best. We got Avengers, we got Suicide Squad but we all know how those games ends up in the end.

The strongest part of this game is off course it's swinging mechanic. Feels so Spider-Man even they can't create such realistic swinging in the movies. Only the web wings is a bit overwhelming because you can just fly from one part of the city to the other like Superman. I'm overall happy with the traversal in this game, even the addition is also brilliant to add some launch point on the top of buildings across the city. The only thing that I want to add if I could is the mechanic where players can get XP from just swinging. The current mechanics felt to monotone.

The combat mechanic still feel the same with the previous tittle. But more gadget added to the armory is a smart additions. And also it's contantlu evolving and adding trough the story. That feeling when you felt stronger as Peter got that symbiote from Harry is fun, or Miles when he got strange blue electric after his first encounter with Mr. Negative. I love that such development to the characters.

Their decision to use 2 characters in one tittle also brilliant, I think Peter will never make it if Miles wasn't there. The enemies is so many with great development in each character. I haven't meet Venom just yet but as far as I can tell, I like Kraven so much and how they add Lizards to the story line deserve a compliment.

The map is bigger now, But I think they need to add more side activity to the game because the amount of side activity is to less compare to how large the game is. Some activity also feel the same with the previous tittle, I just need more than just chasing a bird robot or clearing some enemies lair. They need to think of more side activity if there's the third tittle.

If you ask me would you recommend this game to the other, yes absolutely. You don't have to like Spider-man to play this game. This game is fun, Serious and Mysterious at the same time. The horror vibe when Spider man chasing lizards and when facing Mysterio illusion is something so refreshing. So yes, I would be happy to put this game one of the best Open World game that I've ever played!

Man, what a sad waste of potential in a way. The quality of the cutscenes, animations and voice acting is fantastic, but once again its in the service of a mediocre story, that feels rushed and like it had pieces ripped off of it throughout development. Kraven's role is a mess and he goes out like a wet fart, this version of Venom had a lot of potential but the whole development is rushed and it feels mishandled.
Miles' role in the story should have been far more minimal, his sidequests are ALL Woke fluff and filler that feels like a parody of itself, I'm not kidding, it's that bad. Every time his girlfirend appears on screen, I cringe.
The desperation to install Miles and replace Peter is palpable and I hate it.

The gameplay flows much better than the original, not just combat, but traversal and the fast travel system is impresisve even if I do miss the subways, and would have kept them as as joke option. MY only issue with combat remains that one enemy that you cant find to end the fight because they flew to one distant corner and stayed there killing the flow, but it is very satisfying.
I do also believe upgrades to gadgets should be cheaper so players that dont like doing so much repetitive sidecontent can get more of a look at afuller powerkit.
I still think most MJ sections are pointless (there's only one good one where she is chased by the Symbiote) but at least her equipment makes it more tolerable than the terrible stealth sections of the first game.

They wasted Sandman and Mysterio on sideactivity fluff that I thought were going to have actual promise at the end but are just generic fights and a series of audilogs with a SUPER small cutscene at the end. At least Mysterio gets a boss fight after the quest (short and mid like the previous ones), they probably thought the opening covered Sandman, I wanted more.
And seriously, I cant let go of how every single Miles sidequest is bullcrap. Mister Negative's story with Miles is also very mishandled, feels truncated, and could have been really good if it had more time to develop. Black Cat only has a cameo pretty much, another damned shame. I hope they didnt just assume they would get DLCs and then didnt.
Spider-Sense and powers have been nerfed. MJ Literally reacts faster than Peter in a really stupid scene, both Spiders are surprised constantly, MJs gun hurts enemies more than spider-punches... The power scaling is a bit of a mess.

If you can focus on the fun of the gameplay. and not pay much attention to the massive pile of wasted potential that is everything else, there is fun to be had, and despite the story problems, some set-pieces and sequences are really damn impressive. I’d been waiting more than a year and a half to buy and download Spider-Man 2 on PC. Huge thanks to Sony for skipping PSN finally.

The game itself is incredible. The animations, effects, and everything look stunning - especially those fight sequences when Miles and Peter tag-team enemies. But I did notice a bit of stagnation in the smaller details. What the hell happened to Peter’s new suits? They look rushed - more like pajamas than superhero gear. I’d much rather have just a few amazing outfits than a dozen half-baked ones I’ll never use.

As for the story, I actually preferred the first game. It hit me harder emotionally, felt longer, and kept me on the edge of my seat. Spider-Man 2’s narrative isn’t bad by any stretch, but I was expecting that same level of excitement and depth.

Still, despite that, Spider-Man 2 is amazing. Web-swinging through NYC, pulling off insane combos, and seeing both heroes in action is exactly what I wanted. Highly recommend giving it a spin!

This game is a great improvement from Spider-Man 1 and Miles Morales for sure. Web swinging is so much smoother and they give you a lot more to do while you’re in the air including tricks and other ways to gain speed and traverse faster like the web wings (wingsuit). Peter and Miles both get new powers to use against a wide enemy variety. There are up to 4 or 5 different common enemy types you fight so there’s always a use for a certain power or gadget. Combat feels pretty much the same from miles morales with some obviously new moves and stuff you can do. The story is great I did like it more than the miles morales story but only for a few reasons. The biggest one being how sick the 2 main villains were in this game with the introduction of Kraven in the series and the late arrival of Venom as the main villain. And one of my big gripes with the story is how they kinda just throw away Kraven in less than 10 minutes far into the story to focus on the introduction of Venom. However Kraven as a villain is great I don’t know the lore behind Kraven but as far as I’m concerned he’s just a dude that’s really strong and smart. He doesn’t seem to have any “powers”. Now even though the story is great i have I think like 25-26 hours into the game and I have already beaten the story, all of the FNSM app requests, and most of the activities you can do throughout the map. The story picks up around 6 months after the events of Miles Morales and the underground shenanigans with Pete back in America helping out Miles. And this leads to me complaining how boring and downright pointless some of the side missions are and it would be fine with me if they were fun at all. But a lot of them aren’t. For example a Peter exclusive side quest storyline has you helping a scientist test different devices for green energy and it’s oh so very boring and takes up a decent amount of time for something I’m not there for but for people like me who would like to 100% the game is very annoying. It’s the same for miles but his are more entertaining and have you actually doing Spider-Man stuff through time trials. There are side missions and activities you do that are really fun and actually pertain to the story. A thing I wasn’t too fond and I am never to fond of in superhero media is the corny dialogue that plagues this game. The story is great but sometimes I can’t stand the characters just because of their terrible dialogue. It’s not a big thing but it’s something I noticed. Finally the performance of this game is good but not amazing for PC. I have a decent pc ryzen 7 5800x3D with a 3070 and I ran a constant 70-100fps swinging through the city on very high settings without raytracing around 30-40fps with raytracing. However it crashes for me pretty often and I haven’t found a fix. And I guess for those who are wanting to play on steam deck it’s alright if you tweak the settings a little bit but it’ll run about 30-40fps swinging through the city with frame drops at certain times or if you near a certain part of the map or cutscene. But either way it works and its not bad at all for a handheld. However if you can play it on pc I would try it on pc first before playing it on the steam deck. I would not get it specifically for the steam deck unless it’s on sale.

Overall, a pretty good game but a great story and gameplay with a lot of small bad things that keep the game from reaching full potential. I would recommend this game even if you aren’t into marvel or Spider-Man. And if you want it for the steam deck wait until it’s on sale for under 50 bucks.

This is a much better game than the previous two, and much more ambitious, but it isnt without flaws.

While the presentation and particularly the cutscenes and set-pieces can be jaw-dropping, the music consistently good, the scale of the city overwhelming (in a good way), and best of all the gameplay truly stellar (combat never gets old and neither does traversal nor stealth), I found some parts and the story, specifically revolving about some characters, to be lacking.

There are some small misshaps with the overall story, such as police and army being non-existent (you'd think if a force like the hunters showed up in real life NY there is NO chance they'd be left to do their shenanigans uncontested. In stakes as high as actual world's end, you'd think the avengers would show up, after all their HQ tower is in the middle of the city, but no. I know this is spiderman2, but if you're gonna make the avengers physically present in the game world, then having them do nothing is a little weird.

Those are nitpicks, but the characterization of Pete got on my nerve much more. Him not wanting to kill people at any costs (even when they deserve it and/or it would protect innocents in the long run) is already annoying, but he's also the perfect little labrador puppy, always positive, always humble, always helpful, he's just too perfect. He isn't interesting in any way and has to be (light spoilers that every reader would expect) to start getting an actual personality, flaws, charisma, quite simply some depth. Miles is a much more believable and relatable human being as he actually has flaws, negative emotions as we all do. He's also the GOAT in this game, consistently saves the day, while Peter the labrador is busy apologizing 1500 times to MJ for no reason whatsover. We get it Pete, you're the perfect unicorn boyfriend, like show some teeth and bite when it's needed please.

The devs were also hellbent in making MJ a total badass. I'm sorry but she is just a random ass girl with barely any combat training, probably 50kg at most, but she's just as lethal as both spider-men, this is a bit silly. You can really smell some of the undertones that leaked through the writing. For the most part, this didn't negatively impact the story, but specifically for her character, it sucked. Yes, girl boss muh queen show them girl power wooooooh... boring. This game has plenty of well written female characters that are interesting and/or badass in their own ways, like Hailey (featured in the best side-story in the game) or Rio, there was no need for this parody of MJ.

Other than that, the story was still very enjoyable to follow and got many scenes right, particularly interactions between Harry and Pete, of course Venom stealing the show in every appearance, and Kraven being a nuisance over the whole game but in a great way.

Marvel’s Spider-Man 2 rockets players back into the webbed shoes of Peter Parker and Miles Morales, delivering an exhilarating, emotionally charged superhero adventure that pushes the boundaries of open-world action gaming on recent hardware pc specs.

The narrative masterfully weaves the stories of both Peter and Miles as they face new, formidable threats in New York City. With villains like Venom and Kraven the Hunter entering the fray, the game balances epic set pieces with intimate character moments, exploring themes of responsibility, legacy, and partnership.

The interplay between the two Spider-Men enriches the story, highlighting their contrasting styles, struggles, and growth. It’s a heartfelt journey that honors the comics while offering fresh twists and surprises.

Building on its predecessor’s critically acclaimed mechanics, Spider-Man 2 refines web-swinging to feel even more exhilarating and seamless, with faster speeds and more fluid transitions between traversal and combat.

Combat is deeper and more strategic, introducing new gadgets, abilities, and enemy types. The synergy between Peter’s veteran finesse and Miles’ evolving powers—like bio-electric venom blasts—adds variety and tactical depth to encounters.

Boss battles are bigger, more cinematic, and satisfying, testing players’ mastery of each Spider-Man’s unique skill set.

The game leverages current gen hardware to deliver jaw-dropping visuals, from detailed character models and expressive animations to a vibrant, living New York City filled with dynamic weather and lighting effects.

The soundtrack pulses with energy, perfectly complementing the fast-paced action and emotional beats. Voice acting is stellar, breathing life into beloved characters and bringing authenticity to their relationships.

The open world is packed with diverse activities—from crime-fighting and photo ops to engaging side missions that deepen the lore and character connections. The balance between story-driven missions and fun distractions keeps gameplay fresh and immersive.

Marvel’s Spider-Man 2 is a triumphant sequel that delivers bigger thrills, richer storytelling, and polished gameplay. It’s a must-play for fans of superhero games and anyone looking for a thrilling, heartfelt adventure in one of gaming’s most iconic cities.

An electrifying superhero saga that combines breathtaking action, compelling characters, and a sprawling open world into a seamless next-gen experience.

Pros:
-Thrilling, fluid web-swinging and combat
-Dual protagonist gameplay with unique powers
-Stunning visuals and immersive open world
-Deep, emotional story with well-developed characters
-Engaging side missions and collectibles

Cons:
-Some side content can feel repetitive
-Occasional camera quirks in tight spaces
-High difficulty spikes in late-game boss fights

Rating: 10/10

I am a school teacher and I don't have a ton of time to play games (my steam backlog is nuts thanks to steam sales). I have put about 24 hours into Spider-Man 2 at this point. I really enjoy it when it is running well.... which varies. I have not played a game that has had such a variety of performance issues as this one. I have had to verify all of the files after each update (textures simply disappear). I have both an AMD system and an Nvidia system. Both seem to look great, and play well...for the most part. Here are the issues that have popped up on both systems. The hit boxes seem to randomly change. You can be gliding through the city well clear of buildings and suddenly you are running on the wall because of an "impact". Fights are the same way. Sometimes an opponent seems to hit you from halfway across the screen. This seems to happen more often when the player is low on health. Maybe it is meant to show that when near death your awareness drops? Not sure. Finally, the Prowler missions are another sore spot when it comes to hit boxes and wall impact issues. Your body sticks to everything... when you are invents trying to avoid lasers by precise timing and inexplicably the right side of your body suddenly sticks to the walls. I am not sure if these are bugs or built in by the developers to increase the length of the game, but it sucks the fun out of the level.

All of that being said, I still enjoy the heck out of this game. The game is a ton of fun and the story seems to be pretty good too. I like it and will continue to play and explore the city.

I loved the first game. I did not love this game.

I think a lot of the attention to detail and polish that was present in the first game is absent in this one. For example, in cutscenes there are several instances where a character's mouth will move, the subtitles will include a line, and there will be no audio. I don't know whether this was an instance of my game glitching, or if this was an oversight by the devs, but in either case, nothing like that ever happened during my playthroughs of the first game.

I think the first game did a good job of defining what it felt like to be spider-man, only for this game to stray away from that. Playing as Miles was totally fine, but Peter's new abilities are generic, and the absence of the gadgets really reduced my ability to fight creatively. The missing gadgets also seriously simplified the stealth portions of the game. If Peter had kept his original gadget arsenal, but lacked the generic symbiote abilities, and Miles had his invis and electrical powers, but lacked Peter's gadgets, there would have been a welcome distinction between playing as the two Spider-men

There's less enemy variety in this game, and combat -especially boss combat- feels closer to God of War style combat, where I was waiting for openings, or making themselves with the interrupts caused by my abilities off cooldown. There's none of the first game's arena-interaction bossfights, which I thought was one of the most effective parts of the first game, and helped to distinguish bosses from grunt enemies.

The story is good. I have no complaints about it, although the whole game took my only about 30 hours to beat 100%, whereas the first game took more like 50. I don't mind so much, but I do wonder where the alleged $300M budget went, since the first game had 1/3 the budget, and almost twice the content, and certainly assets from the first game were reused in this one.

Lastly the performance issues: They still persist, even now. It's frustrating. I have a 3070 Ti, and still was not able to get the game running smoothly, even on low settings. I have a hard time understanding why this is, because the game is not meaningfully better looking than its predecessor. It's just optimized worse.

I recommend this game only if you really enjoyed the first game, are aware that this is certainly not the masterpiece that the first game was, and have a computer with specs much much better than the recommended specs, and have an easier time running it than I did.

Unless you are a hardcore Spider-man fan and are just willing to muscle through it, I would not suggest buying this on PC. It is poorly optimized and has consistent graphics glitches even after all the patches to fix them. Playing with a 4060TI for reference point and have used several tools to "optimize" gameplay for my system and still after playing for an hour each time the same bug reoccurs.

Marvel’s Spider-Man 2 is overall an amazing game. The web-swinging mechanics through the city are incredibly well done and super fun. The story is engaging and keeps you hooked from start to finish. On a technical level, the game is very well optimized—I had absolutely no issues on my system, and the graphics look fantastic. However, there are a few drawbacks: the achievement system once again requires you to replay the entire game on a specific difficulty level, which can be a bit frustrating, and there are a lot of collectibles that, while not bad in themselves, do become a bit repetitive over time.

Everything I felt was lacking in the first games:

- Too much repetitive side content
- Being a bit too long
- Side content like crimes and such having lower and lower incentive to do them since you didn't get much from them, or what you did get wasn't really needed to upgrade any further
- Etc.

All of these were improved in Miles Morales and practically erased in Spider-Man 2.

This game from start to finish was so much fun and so much better in terms of:

- Travel
- Combat
- Side Quests
- Action
- Cinematography
- So much more

To me its a 9/10.

The only reason I don't give it a 10/10 is because I reserve that for stories that make a massive impact on me personally and this being a bit light hearted doesn't really fill that need.

But otherwise, a proper classic.

If you liked the first 2 games, you're gonna love this game. As a semi-casual fan (Watched all Spider-Man movies, but never read any comics) some parts in this game got me HYPED. So hyped that I paused the game and yelled (through text) at my friend that he HAD to play this game himself and to stay spoiler-free before that.

Seriously, if you're considering this game, try to avoid any spoilers.

I see a lot of negative reviews talking about performance: It ran as well as any other game runs for me, which is to say, mostly well, but sometimes frames drop at graphically intensive scenes, which mostly means anything with a lot of reflections. Granted, I played with Raytracing reflections (I enjoy the realism), but you absolutely don't need raytracing to play this game. I have an RTX 3070Ti if that's important information. My graphics were set to a mixture of very high and ultra.

The game crashed twice throughout my entire playthrough, and relatively early as well, but with the generous checkpoint system, it barely bothered me. Oh right, and sometimes the world would take some time to load during cutscenes. But honestly, I'm SO used to modern games being like that that I barely registered that as an issue until I read a negative review talking about it. I mean, just pause, wait for a few seconds, then resume: the textures should load soon enough. It is what it is. What are you gonna do about it? Wait ad infinitum until the game is patched to perfection? It's good enough. You're better off getting the game now / on sale, and playing it now.

Additionally, it's a relatively short game, and I LIKE that. As a working adult, I don't have a lot of time to play games. And games that are too long make me lose interest in them. Spider-Man 2 was perfect. I even had some spare time to achievement hunt.

I highly suggest—when you play—to turn down the swing-steering assist to 1 (default is 10, max), or 0 if you feel adventurous. You feel like you're actually swinging on buildings rather than hovering through the streets. 1 still gives you a little bit of a cushion to not skid on the ground; While 0, if you touch the ground, that's it, you lose your swing, which makes chases difficult. Also, there is no achievement for playing on the hardest difficulty; don't repeat my mistake. Play on the hardest difficulty only because you want to; there is no reward for it.

Spider-Man 2 – A Great Game Held Back by Its PC Performance

Visually stunning, mechanically refined, and full of high-flying action—Spider-Man 2 builds on what came before. Traversal is noticeably faster and smoother, and new abilities make getting around New York more fun than ever. Combat feels familiar but gets a nice boost with added powers that deepen the gameplay, though the new parry system feels out of place for a character as agile as Spider-Man.

The story, sadly, doesn't quite live up to the high bar set by the original game or Miles Morales. It tries to juggle too many narratives—Peter, Miles, and the main villain arcs—all at once, and ends up spreading itself thin. It’s not bad, just… fine.

However, the biggest issue is the PC performance. Frequent crashes and FPS drops made the game frustrating to play at times. Optimization feels like an afterthought, and that’s a real shame for such a high-profile release.

Final Verdict:
Definitely worth playing—if your system can run it well. Otherwise, maybe wait for some patches.

As a Spiderman fan, I really liked the game mechanics. It really added a lot to the first game. They reduced the unused web features and added a few important ones. Venom's powers are fun to play with. However, the second half of the game, the Venom part, was very boring. I was expecting more scenes with Venom and epic fights. But despite all that, it's great as a superhero and a Spiderman game. I hope we get to see more influence from our main villain in the next game.

Moment-to-moment gameplay is fantastic. Combat is super-fun with the notable exception that the targeting is super wonky and can be frustrating. As with the first game, just swinging around nyc alone is fantastic. I 100%ed every zone and had fun during most of the time that I could play the game.

The problem is that I spent a ton of time trying to get the game to run rather than playing the game. The technical state of the game is simply unacceptable. Frequent crashes, and the game would lock up on startup more often than not. The only fix was to restart my computer and hope it would work after that. Sometimes it would, sometimes it wouldn't. I tried verifying the files and fully reinstalling the game to no avail. Some days I just couldn't play the game at all. My hardware is more than up to snuff (when it runs, it runs well at fairly high settings with ray tracing).

The story and dialogue are a big step down from the first game as well. I'm not offended by the politics or anything, but the writing and dialogue just feel like someone phoned it in. The first game was pretty good in this department, so it's disappointing to see them drop the ball here. Granted, if this were the only issue, the gameplay could still carry this one if it weren't for the technical issues. Despite the poor dialogue, the voice actors did a great job with what they had to work with.

If the technical issues get patched out, this will 100% be worth a buy on sale, even if it is a bit of a step down from the first game , but until then I can't recommend it.

I would recommend this game to people who loved the combat and traversal of Spider-Man Remastered or Spider-Man Miles Morales as the developers did an incredible job of improving those mechanics from the already good mechanics from the previous games. Spider-Man 2 is a good game — in some areas, even a great one — but it’s definitely not the masterpiece many of us were hoping for after the first game.

Let’s start with the positives: the swinging feels smoother and more dynamic than ever. Moving through the city is genuinely fun, and the upgraded graphics and expanded map make it all feel bigger and better. The new suits? Easily some of the best in the series. And the boss fights are more intense and creative this time around, with some real standout moments.

The game starts strong too — the opening hours of the story are engaging and set the stage well. But the deeper you get into it, the more rushed it all feels. The pacing completely falls apart toward the end, and it’s hard not to feel like the second half of the game was on fast-forward. The ending, in particular, just didn’t land for me. It felt abrupt and lacked emotional weight.

A big part of that problem comes down to the relationship between Peter and Harry. The game clearly wants you to feel the pain of watching two best friends fall apart, but how can we as players feel that when we only get, what, three meaningful missions with them together? There’s no real time to build that bond — not in the way they nailed Peter and Otto in the first game. It makes Harry’s transformation and the emotional fallout feel unearned.

Also, while the game is fun to play, it reuses a lot from the first one — same animations, same type of side missions over and over. At a certain point it starts to feel like filler.

In the end, Spider-Man 2 is still a blast to play and a worthy sequel in a lot of ways — better visuals, better gameplay, and some great moments. But it could’ve been so much more if the story had taken its time. The rushed narrative and weak ending stop it from reaching the heights of the original.

*no story spoilers but I do spoil some new game mechanics*

If you liked SM1 and MM then you will almost certainly enjoy this game as well because it's mostly more of the same. As of May, 2025 it's been pretty playable on PC and I only experienced a couple big crashes during my two full play-throughs but it might vary between systems.

As far as game-play goes, what's great about the other titles is still great here.

Web swinging through NYC is incredibly satisfying especially as you level up and unlock more swinging abilities. The map is also the best yet and the addition of the wing-suit makes traversal even faster if you want it to be. I do appreciate how the wing-suit is entirely optional for the most part and you can get everywhere just by good ole fashioned web swinging if you want.

The combat is also very similar to the other two games. Combos, rechargeable abilities, gadgets and perfect dodges are all here with the addition of the parry. Enemies now have a new telegraph for heavy attacks that you'll need to parry instead of dodge. While I think this is a good addition to the combat in theory, I found the timing and controls just didn't feel tight enough for parrying to feel satisfying. Oftentimes I thought I had hit parry at the right time but because I was stuck in a previous move's animation my parry wouldn't register and I'd take a ton of damage. I found myself mostly just jumping and web zipping away from heavy attacks to avoid them instead of trying to parry.

The story that you play through in this game is what we've come to expect from Insomniac Spider-Man games. It's corny, idyllic and doesn't really make you think too much but that's okay. It has some fun set pieces, there are some endearing moments and they finally gave MJ a little more to do in her sections which is a welcome addition. The story was enjoyable and exactly what I expected. It won't blow your mind but it's good fun.

Now for my one complaint about this game: the boss battles. I found the boss battles in this game to be tedious and not enjoyable at all. I wouldn't say these bosses are difficult because even on spectacular difficulty, this is not a particularly hard game. What I found frustrating about these battles was the design. For a lot of the bosses I felt that the arena you fight them in is just too small and feels cramped. I would often find myself getting stuck in places around the arena and then getting hit hard because I couldn't move which is always frustrating. The camera is also horrendous during boss battles. It does not move fast enough to keep up with the combat and I was constantly losing sight of the bosses as I would web zip and swing around to avoid attacks. I also didn't like how the boss phases were designed. Every boss has at least two phases with some of the bigger ones having up to four. You hack away at a boss until their health bar is at zero and then they enter their next phase and their health bar fully resets. I hate this! I don't why games do this! Just have the health bar decrease slowly and introduce the different phases at 3/4 health, 1/2 health etc. so that the battle actually does end when the bosses health is at zero. There's nothing worse then finally finishing a boss phase after multiple attempts only to see their health bar fully refill. It feels like you've accomplished nothing.

Overall, I would definitely recommend this game to those who enjoyed the first two titles. I think this is a solid evolution of the other games. I also think new players will enjoy this as well and because the stories are are pretty straight forward, you won't really miss a ton by not playing the other games first. You're Spider-Man, you have to save the day, that's all you really need to know. My gripes about the boss battles are pretty minor and I wouldn't let them scare you off. Like I said, the boss battles aren't that hard so they most likely won't stop your play through dead in it's tracks. I just found them to be annoying and was disappointed by the execution. I did my first play through on spectacular difficulty and then did another new game+ play through on the unlocked ultimate difficulty which still isn't particularly hard but it is fun to re-play the beginning of the game at max level and just wreck house. I'm not typically the type of person to re-play a game immediately so the fact that I did with this one says a lot about the overall experience. It's just good arcade-ey fun!

I think Marvel's Spider-Man 2 is the best Spider-Man game. In combat and exploration they've done an amazing job of creating an experience that feels genuine to the character. In combat you feel super quick and strong, the abilities are fun, and major improvements have been made to the gadgets. In prior games it felt like gadgets were a throw-in meant for stealth (and potentially to mimic what people are expecting post-Arkham games), but they felt out of place for the Spider-Man character and were more tedious to use and create. Here they feel like unique abilities that you're able to chain into combos. Exploration feels amazing - in prior games New York felt a little lifeless; even though the scale was awesome, there weren't many people around doing normal things, cars, highway, boats, the list goes on... but here the city has so much going on. I do think they could improve even more on this front though, I distinctly remember a moment where I was standing on the street looking through a window, and watched an NPC walk down the stairs and out of the building. I was super impressed but noticed the poster on the door stayed floating in place when the door itself was opened. Also had a few instances of floating NPCs. While I'm on things I enjoyed, I've got to mention the boss fights. They represent characters well, and created some awesome fights with a decent level of challenge to them. The side missions and open-world activities are also massively improved here, they are a lot more fun, a lot less tedious, and often tie into the main story, or set-up potential DLC / follow-up games.

The writing was hit-or-miss for me. I'm okay with the super comic-book inspired style, and I think they nailed a few familial moments here (Also nailed a few humorous moments). That being said, there are significantly long portions of the main story where Peter, Harry, and MJ spend time together, in those moments and throughout the game, the way they interact feels like they only know each other at a surface level, completely failing to sell the relationships they have. Time is also confusing within the plot, feels like some events are a day apart, and weeks apart at the same time, it's addressed inconsistently. There are also more MJ missions, more flashback sequences, and more non-Spider-Man sequences. They're not terrible, but they're so much less fun than running around as Spider-Man that you'd like them to be significantly more enjoyable if you must do them.

Lastly, I've got to mention the performance given how bad the launch was. I waited four months (10 patches in that time), and the game was gorgeous and fully playable at solid FPS most of the time, but I still encountered many issues. The game still needs more patches, and if you want the optimal experience in you should continue waiting. The goal with these ports must be excellence, not "OK" and my PC is certainly strong enough to run this much better. I experienced one full crash, and dozens of moments of significant stuttering/screen tearing and FPS drops (Still playable, but annoying). I also commonly experienced a bug where certain voice lines wouldn't play (both in cut-scenes, and during gameplay), for example, Peter and MJ having a conversation, but only the MJ lines play audio.

Ultimately, I'd recommend , it's a great game. I love the movement options, chaining them together while zipping across New York feels amazing. Combat is great, you feel lightning-fast, and powerful as Spider-Man should feel. At times the writing and non-Spider-Man sections detract from the experience, but overall it's still a wonderful time (especially if your expectation is "great game", and not "GOTY 2023").

“This is where we became best friends; And now it's where we become brothers”

Taking place after the events of Spider-Man and Spider-Man: Miles Morales , Peter and Miles have been protecting the city as a Spider-Man duo when all of a sudden a new villain known as Kraven the Hunter leading a group called the Hunters shows up and starts kidnapping New-York's supervillains. Meanwhile, Peter's best friend, Harry, resurfaces out of treatment for his illness carrying a friend that might shatter the very image of what it means to be Spider-Man.

Spider-Man 2 is a great game held back by its early pacing and a largely superfluous open world. The main storyline is gripping, the voicework is top-notch, the visuals are excellent, the fighting is fun. So there is a lot to love here, and most of it you would probably love from the original. This expanded vision of New York City opens with the unfortunate fact that Kraven has come to town and is busy hunting down current and former supervillains as he attempts to find the fight of his life. Obviously you know that Peter gets his own symbiote powers, which is when the game begins to quickly go from a bit dull to much more fun, and then when Venom himself arrives (in a truly wild sequence I will not spoil), the game floors the gas and becomes incredible in its third act.

It is hard to explain just how insane the game gets during the Venom segment without spoilers. Suffice to say that this realization of Venom is exceptional in every way, from the character itself to his rather horrifying effects on the city and its population. Venom in this game might just be the best non-comic adaptation of the character I’ve seen. They portray so well just how unbelievably powerful the symbiote is. And Tony Todd as the voice of Venom is phenomenal and gave me goosebumps.

I do think that one of Spider-Man 2’s weakest aspects is the open world. Marketing showed off the dramatically expanded map with more boroughs including Brooklyn and such across the water. But a bigger city mainly just means that things are…more spread out. Unless you live in NYC and know all the landmarks, as beautiful as this rendered version of the city may be, it just sort of blends together as you speed through various locales. And one new aspect about the city, because it’s so big, you will be doing a lot less swinging and using the new “spider wings” more, complete with airflow wind tunnels you use to get around quickly. It’s fine, but it felt a little odd to be swinging half as much as before.

But that said, my appreciation for the game slowly built up steam over time, and by the end, I cannot say that at least the main string of missions that barrels toward the end of the game is anything other than brilliant and intensely memorable. It’s one of my favorite Spider-Man sequences…ever, in any medium. I just wish it didn’t take quite so long to reach that point, and that the Kraven bits were as compelling.

For fans of the first game, or Spider-Man, or action games in general, Spider-Man 2 is an easy recommendation. There are about five dozen teasers and easter eggs by the end so I’m sure we’re in for more, whether that’s another Miles-sized game or another sequel down the road. But for now, yes, this is all the Spider-action you need for a good long while.

Don't get me wrong: The game, the story, everything within is absolutely fantastic. I don't need to mention that if you are a fan of Spiderman, this game is a MUST.

That being said, the game is very poorly optimized. I've seen here and there reviews from people saying that the lack of optimization is a lie and they disagree: Well, to those people, it seems they lack understanding on how the PC world works. It is not the same to make a game for PS5, than port it to PC with the amount of different hardware combinations. For some, it works well, for others, we suffer lots of issues not matter how expensive or extensive is our machine.

I SHOULD be able to run this game properly, is not rocket science, but I faced the following:

- Stutterings, some very minimal, but some would lead to me being killed (fighting at 120 fps and then suddently having an unexpected drop to 5 fps with 5 guys and 2 projectiles coming at me)
- Random Freezings (which led me to force close the game through task manager)
- Video not syncing with the audio (or viceversa)
- I reached the end of the game, and cinematics that were suppossed to be mindblowing ended up being utterly destroyed because the game would "freeze" the scene, but it was not a freeze of the game itself but rather a freeze of the characters, as you could see that the physics of their clothes and other movable objects kept moving while everyone was stuck in their place floating in the air.

And at one point, at the end of a mission very advanced in the game, I even got BIG BLUE SQUARES where the UI should be, covering most of the screen, not being able to see anything.

Sometimes i think there is a memory leak, because on some occasion i opened up the task manager and found out that the game was consuming 100% of the CPU. The game starts perfectly, but as time goes by, performance begins to fall until a point where I even had to restart the computer.

Again - PC world is a vast one, and some people may had have no problems at all (which I'm happy for them, honestly), but this is one of the very unique occasions where my computer struggles to keep this going. So if it works for you, AWESOME! GREAT! ENJOY IT!, but don't silence people like me who cannot enjoy the experience properly because of lots and lots of issues which are clearly part of lack of optimization for different types of gigs.

I will definitely change my review once i see some improvement in the performance. Until then, hear me out: If you are lucky and the game runs well on YOUR PC, go blindly for it. It is awesome. If you are experiencing issues, then wait for a proper patch.

Spider-Man 2 is Sony's followup to 2018's successful Spider-Man, and it is in most respects a classic iterative sequel, delivering a bigger story with more cinematic set pieces, gameplay improvements, and more life-like New York to swing around. This time, you can switch between Miles Morales and Peter Parker, and although they play similarly, Miles has electrical abilities that differentiate his powers from Peter's, who goes through much of the game with the symbiote suit. Kraven the hunter is the main antagonist for about two-thirds of the runtime, until Venom shows up, with much fanfare. Sandman and the Lizard also make notable appearances, with the former appearing at the start of the game and more or less recreating his gigantic form from Sam Rami's Spider-Man 3. The story involves the dual Spider-Men attempting to prevent Kraven from offing their rogue's gallery, until the appearance of Harry Osborn, who has kept his life-threatening illness at bay with a biological suit of unknown origins, diverts Pete's attention. You can probably predict the rest, but playing through Spider-Man 2 is at least as enjoyable as watching one of his classic flicks. I have been thoroughly pursuing the sidequests, not just because of their quality (do the one involving Mysterio's virtual reality game) but because it's just plain fun to traverse this hyper-detailed New York. Both Pete and Miles have web-wings which allows them to fly alongside their web-slinging, and it is thrilling to glide in-between towering skyscrapers and across New York Bay. High quality ray-traced reflections look excellent and really add to the immersion. Combat has been expanded since the first game, with both Spider-Men having special abilities that they tap into during battle along with an array of gadgets and combo moves. Two-thirds in, you'll really master the system and start handling massive mobs with the supernatural dexterity of a spider-powered hero. All in all, Spider-Man 2 is right up there with God of War: Ragnarok as one of the best singleplayer games I've experienced this year.

A note on the PC version; it's not quite as good as you would really expect from Sony and Nixxies, who did the port. When it was released earlier this year, it was full of crashes and bugs and had terrible performance on even high-end hardware. A couple months later, the bugs aren't really plentiful (I got stuck behind level geometry a couple of times), but Spider-Man 2 still crashes more than is really acceptable. There's nothing that really prompts the crashes; sometimes I'll play for an hour or more and it will crash, other times just a few minutes. As for performance, this game is ridiculously CPU-heavy with ray-tracing enabled, especially when you consider how the Playstation 5 sports a down-clocked Zen 2 CPU. A smooth sixty frames per second is not possible without frame generation on my hardware. However, FSR frame gen can be enabled along with Nvidia's Reflex anti-lag tech and DLSS upscaling, and the result is pretty good. I played the game with an RTX 3080 and a Ryzen 7 5800x with a mix of High and Very High settings, with ray-traced reflections and interiors enabled on Very High at 1440p DLSS Quality, and my frame rates varied between 120 and 80 fps. I didn't notice any input lag or visual bugs due to frame gen, so I'd definitely enable it.

In conclusion, Spider-Man 2 looks great and plays great, but asks for some serious hardware and requires frame generation to run smoothly on PC. It think it's worth the asking price at this point, but it is a shame that Nixxes couldn't port the title to PC without some hurdles. Screenshots below:

I absolutely love this game. It has an absolutely amazing story that picks up from the previous installments in the story. This is for sure my favorite game to come out recently.

Look, I know what you're thinking...

"Wow! Spider-Man! Swings across the skyline like a caffeinated Tarzan! Must love heights!"

Well, surprise: I have the same relationship with heights that cats have with bathtubs. I don't scream, but there's a lot of internal panicking and awkward flailing.

So when Spider-Man 2 asked me to leap off the Empire State Building like a Red Bull-sponsored squirrel, I politely screamed like a grandma on a rollercoaster and plummeted face-first into a hotdog cart. 10/10 immersion.

But let’s talk about the real villain of this game. Not Venom. Not Kraven.

Constipation.

Yep. Being a superhero is stressful. Crime never sleeps, and neither does his intestinal confusion. You ever tried taking a bathroom break in a skintight suit with no zippers? My digestive system is like a haunted house—mysterious noises, unexpected jump scares, and a strong desire to escape.

By the end of the game, I had conquered my fears—both aerial and intestinal. I'm a new man. A braver man. A man who now carries emergency prunes in his utility belt.

10/10. Would poop my pants again for justice.

This is a worthy follow-up with some performance issues, It really needs to run off an SSD with background tasks shut down. Occasional glitching in cutscenes (especially in the final act) with sound sync issues. Occasional freezes requiring a reset. Running on 32 RAM with a 4060 GTX.

Webbing round New York is a fun as it always was, although I'm not convinced on some of the mini-missions (spider-bots, trying to get high as you can to deploy min-satellites) plus some cut-scenes can get in the way of the action - do we *really* need to help Peter clean up his house by pressing "X" to put things in the bin? Or wander round the 'May' foundation? Or have a flashback to Harry and Peters schooldays?

Combat is top notch (kudos to the web-line mechanic and sneaky take-downs) and great fun with some truly spectacular set-pieces; Fighting bad guys with a story companion really nails those comic book vibes. If I had a criticism its that after 8 hours in Kraven's army of goons get
a little tedious and the story pacing feels off. The forced switches between Miles and Peter feel jarring (after my last scrap with Kraven's goons I was slightly annoyed to be snapped out of Peter's story back into Miles narrative). By act 3 with endless symbiotes and ridiculous area of effect powers things are about the level of a Michael Bay film with so much chaos it can be difficult to work out what is going on. Depending on your point of view the final fight is either epic - or just too drawn out. Personally I was beginning to tire of its multiple sections. But when the narrative works (and it works a lot of the tiime) - this is full of comic book goodness.

For nostalgia factor and roster of villains I still lean towards 'remastered'. But for bombastic set pieces and boss fights this stands head and shoulders above it. The Lizard showdown is spot on as is the face-off with Kraven the Hunter. This is still an excellent follow up and a worthy adaption of the Spiderman universe. If your PC is up to the task give it a swing.

After playing both the first two insomniac games on PlayStation, I ended up waiting for this to release on PC and for space in my schedule to play it, after a bit over 20hrs (reaching 96% on Amazing difficulty) I can say that they have definitely continued their great work with these 2 amazing web-slingers.

Throughout my game-time I did not experience any crashes, noticeable stutters or performance issues, and reaching a pretty consistent 250 fps on all high settings. I wouldn't say I have a mega-setup either so I'm not entirely sure why my experience differs from some that have had issues.

RTX 4070
Intel Core i5-13600KF
32GB RAM
Windows 11

I have only 3 gripes that I can think of after completing the game, but they're pretty subjective;

- Sandman's voice, can't tell if it's the after-effects or the performance but you end up listening to him waffle a good bit doing his missions and it was pretty tedious and uninteresting. The missions can be ignored and you can limit the exposure to just the beginning, but I wanted to complete all the side-missions I could along the way and I found myself always tuning out. :(

- I found Kraven to be underwhelming as he didn't really show me how he is an expert hunter, other than being an over-sized strongman (which there are numerous of), I never really felt like his prey. Without completely spoiling story bits, :/

- featured in the flame based side missions for Peter felt a bit strange to me, as I don't recall too much of their role in the previous titles and yet they have seemingly had a pretty big change coming into this one. The feats and actions they do don't seem to be explained or make much sense for what they should be capable of and the actual character themselves did not stick the landing for me. ;) (although I could just be a forgetful idiot and this is very much elaborated on previously)

Other than those takes, this is a great follow-up on the game series, featuring one of my favourite characters being which I was quite happy with the take-on. By the end there are plenty of fun abilities, additions to movement and QoL which don't go amiss.

I'm looking forward to completing this again on NG+ soon.

Pros:
- Great boss fights. No need to play the waiting game as if it's a gimmick fight, instead you can bring the fight to the boss using something from your arsenal.
- Much improved traversal compared to previous games with a decent variety too (web wings, web swinging, super launches, mid-air jumps and dashes, etc)
- Great story which is more than just a white boy fantasy and makes sure to shine plenty of light on Miles, MJ, Rio, etc. Ganke could still use some more screen-time.

Cons:
- Poor optimisation on PC
- Too expensive for the 35 hour runtime to 100% the game
- No upcoming DLCs and tons of loose ends in the sidequests

TL;DR: A mediocre good game, that may have worked pretty well as its own thing, but fails really badly as a sequel to the masterpiece that was Spiderman 1.
If you love Miles and don't care much for Peter, you'll enjoy the story (maybe).
If you like Peter equally or more, the story is just bad imo.

Peter is completely carried in the entire story, mostly by Miles. I cannot think of literally a single major thing Peter solved on his own -- even in the side stories he was a failure. SPOILER(-ish): For fans of the first game, this won't feel like a Peter who fought the Sinister Six all on his own.

As for the , well they removed suit special abilities, heavily reduced gadget variations, and reduced combo variations (atleast it feels like it) in favor of Super attacks, which is a new mechanic (I think, can't remember this being in the existing game). And so the gameplay is built around spamming Supers as quickly as you can, which feels much less rewarding than pulling off cool combos using actual button inputs and combining suit abilities into your playstyle.

, she appears early in the story, gets a magical portal wand and teleports to Paris to be with her gf. Only interacts with Miles and MJ, so you don't get the sultry part at all. So yeah.

I'm not even going into MJ's model redesign or how bad Miles' exposed hair looks in his final suit -- or that his final suit literally has no story (that I know of). He just randomly designed a new suit in the middle of a city-wide crisis.

Better than the previous two iterations. Great story line even if it deviates from the original material. Sometimes when calls cut out because you entered a fight (intended mechanic) they do not resume when the fight is over. I 100%'ed all districts at the time of writing (25 hrs). So I does not take super long to play.

Marvel's Spider-Man 2 improves the already good combat gameplay of their predecessors: Marvel's Spider-Man and Marvel's Spider-Man: Miles Morales. The addition of the abilities tree offer more diversity that expand the way that you can fight in the game, making the combat more dynamic and fun. The MJ missions are improved from the first game: now you have the opportunity to fight back and play a more aggressive stealth gameplay. The customization of the spider-men are improved too: the suits are nicer and more stylish than ever. The game is very fun to play, the characters are interesting and the story is a solid one. Had a lot of fun playing it. It's a 9/10 game.

Marvel’s Spider-Man 2 is an action-adventure video game released in 2023 for the PlayStation 5, developed by Insomniac Games. It continues the story of Peter Parker and Miles Morales as they team up to protect New York City from powerful new threats. The game introduces Kraven the Hunter, who arrives in the city with an army to hunt superpowered beings, and Venom, a monstrous transformation of Peter after he bonds with the alien symbiote. As Peter struggles with the symbiote's dark influence, Miles takes on more responsibility, and the two Spider-Men must work together to stop chaos from spreading. The game features fast-paced combat, upgraded abilities, and seamless web-swinging across a larger, more detailed open-world version of New York City, including new areas like Queens and Brooklyn.
